title: Lattice Gauge Theory and Wilson-Loop Confinement: A Statistical-Mechanical Survey
authors: Ethan Zhou, Marcus Reed, Caleb Hayes
abstract: Wilson loops provide the central gauge-invariant probe of confinement in lattice gauge theory. This survey reviews the statistical-mechanical formulation of lattice gauge ensembles, the strong-coupling and duality mechanisms behind area laws, finite-temperature and continuum scaling diagnostics, and the mathematical status of Wilson-loop confinement.
summary: This survey provides a comprehensive overview of how confinement in lattice gauge theory can be understood as a statistical-mechanical problem of surface tension and topological defects. It connects various theoretical frameworks, such as dualities and effective string models, to the behavior of Wilson loops. The paper highlights the ongoing mathematical challenge of proving the existence of a mass gap in 4D non-Abelian theories.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism of confinement in lattice gauge theory through the lens of statistical mechanics, specifically how the area law arises and the mathematical challenge of proving a continuum mass gap.
main_result: The survey synthesizes various viewpoints—including center vortices, monopoles, and dualities—to show that confinement is a statistical-mechanical phenomenon related to surface tension and extended topological defects.
method: The paper reviews theoretical frameworks such as strong-coupling expansions, character expansions, duality transformations, and numerical Monte Carlo techniques like multilevel algorithms.
model_or_system: Lattice Gauge Theory (LGT) formulated as a statistical mechanics of parallel transport on a Euclidean hypercubic lattice, including SU(N) Yang-Mills, Z2 gauge theory, and compact U(1) models.
key_observables: Wilson loops, Polyakov loops, 't Hooft loops, Creutz ratio, string tension, and the mass gap.
important_parameters: Coupling constant (beta), number of colors (N), temperature (T), lattice spacing (a), and N-ality.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the continuum limit and relies on the convergence of expansions like the strong-coupling or cluster/polymer expansions.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the statistical-mechanical formulation of LGT, moves through strong-coupling and duality mechanisms, discusses numerical diagnostics and error analysis, explores finite-temperature transitions and universality, and concludes with the mathematical status of the continuum limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
